regions.text: The Negro in Politics

regions.label: text
regions.text:

a letter to the Rev. August 14, 1904 and the LS Transport Branch of Roosevelt wrote. The b and Tent's story fought on the battle of the age and that he be beside me in the Dene in the showened cell of the army when I shall take the compass I shall tell them "TH. SHADOW ROOSEVELT COL"

I this is history Roosevelt has id in part on the stage and is so the Roosevelt ideals, in so had power the b and Tent the that they b and Tent in his good intentions his sense of justice and gratitude we are outlid the breastworks Mr Harding is not a Roosevelt in resemble Grant and never was Lincoln The Negro question with is considered a pestiferous nuile and he found a solution for it imingham. Ala. when he advised way out that we divide our votes even the two great parties and thus our own political salvation ligh these medias We are going to keep on doing it having id the Republican Party for fifty years and ceased in these years to be useful to it The o has been knocked out of it by

regions.label: text
regions.text:
President Harding and the Lily White annexes of the party to "root hog or die." He's going to root from now on. He was a good enough Morgan in critical times before tariffs, ship subsidies and other big questions became so important to the life of the party of Lincoln and Grant and Roosevelt and the dollar a more vital factor than the Man. Now, the Negro is merely an incident, a political bump on a log—sans influence, sans power, sans standing among the various group elements which now constitute the great Republican Party. He was originally the ladder on which it climbed to the dizzy heights to which it has since attained The Hon. W. G. Harding kicked down the ladder at Birmingham, Ala., and made us political outcasts. Maybe in 1923 or 1924 somebody who used to know us well in the old days will be locking for "we."

From the conversation I hear in various odd spots of Harlem the wet plank in the Democratic platform will have the same value to Al Smith as four acres in a skillful game of poker. The promise of light wines and beer which these few remarks hold out to the dry legions without sound and the dry regionals without sound and the race, party or color "will endeavor or try" to make the vote for Alfred unanimous. The issue with them is not one of politics, but of a long continued and aridty which has almost made the process of deglutition impossible even with the use of plain water. If Mr. Smith wins (and you'd better watch him), he will be ennobized by the wets the country over their savior. There is much in the wet plank to give the Republicans pause.

All other issues important enough in themselves are subordinate to the voter who wants light wine, and beer. This has been a long-felt want, and thirsty thousands who want what they want when they want it are not going to let the opportunity pass to put themselves aquately on record with the Democratic Party in favor of modifying an odious law which has cut off the poor man's beer and the householders light wine—both of them good and harmless juices. Thus the popularity of Al Smith is easily explained. He looks like a comer I may be mistaken, but I doubt it.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: "Black 8am"

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Francese's Tavern, in Thames street, in the lower part of New York City, was built in 1710 by Ettienne DeLaney for his own residence. It became the property of his grandson, Oliver DeLaney, and about 1757 was used for business purposes and occupied by the firm of DeLaney Robinson & Co. In 1762 it was purchased by Samuel Fraunces, a West Indian Negro of mixed blood, and called "Black Sam," who established an inn called the "Queen's Head. The New York Chamber of Commerce was organized there in 1768. It was a meeting place for the Sons of Liberty prior to the Revolution, and as such became the target for the British ship Asia in 1775 after the seizure by a party of King's College students, led by Alexander Hamilton, a West Indian man of color from the island of Nevis, who took a quantity of guns and ammunition from the port at Bowling Green.

Samuel Fraunces allied himself with the patriotic cause, enlisting in Col. Malcolm's regiment. When the British entered New York he abandoned the tavern, returning only in time to prepare a banquet given by Governor George Clinton to general Washington, his officers and prominent citizens upon the triumphal entry of the Americans on November 25, 1783. It was here also that Washington bade farewell to his officers. One hundred years later the society of the Sons of the Revolution was reorganized in the famous long room of the tavern.

Samuel Fraunces, "Black Sam," was, as I have said, a Negro of mixed blood. He was said, the Negro of his day. Care has been taken by the press of the present day to conceal the fact of the Negro origin. The newspapers of his day however, did not hesitate to say that he was a man of color. He was a first-class cook and caterer, and his house was the resort of aristocratic clubmen, bankers, army officers, merchants and private citizens of note. Fraunces was to the New York elite what Wormley was to Washington He had the cream of the trade because he was at that time the best cook in the city. The wonder is that some combination of colored men familiar with the intricacies of the catering business do not break into the downtown district, and bring American cooking back to its pristine glory and toothsomeness. There is undoubtedly much money in it, for with the vision and the art to properly tickle the American palate and convert and dedicate it again to the American way of eating, the American style of cooking, with which the Southern Negro cook is a past master, could again take its place in the first anks. In those early days dyspepsia as practically unknown. People were euphilier and happier and enjoyed their meals and lived longer, and grub was called by its proper name. Now most everybody in the fashionable eating places eat in French and half of them never know what they are putting in their "innards."

regions.label: text
regions.text:
is going to be close enough for each of them to feel that there was "something doing" during the voting. The wet plank in the Democratic platform, on which Mr. Smith stands flatfootedly, will assuredly bring to his standard all that element (and they are legion) who believe that the people ought to have light wines and beer. If Mr. Smith wins on this issue the thin edge of the wedge will have been placed under the Volstead law in this State and the United Order of Amalgamated Hootchetarians will have by their votes registered a protest which will inspire those in other communities to go and likewise in future campaigns and thus make possible a nation-wide demand by the electorate for light wines and beer.

I fancy that many Republican wets will vote for Al Smith on this issue because it is vital to them, and its success will give hope of larger benefits to come and which they will work zealously to obtain. I think the Volstead act is a piece of puritanic legislation which can never be fully enforced while it occupies a place on the statute books. It has done more harm than good, made as many, if not more, criminals of one sort and another than were made under the old order, and cost the taxpayers more money to enforce its provisions than it cost the government to run the Internal Revenue Department before the law was enacted. The taxpayer does not derive any benefit from its enforcement. He is merely permitted to help foot the bills to carry out its provisions and read about the destruction of thousands of dollars' worth of perfectly good and drinkable liquor

There are apparently as many drunkards as ever, and more tragic deaths from imbibing home brew than were known before the Volstead act became a law; more illicit manufacturers of liquid poison, which cost the victims a higher rate than was paid for the old brands of gin, whiskey and brandy which are now made over night and warranted to kill in from 24 to 48 hours, according to the amount taken into the system. The Volstead act has made possible the manufacture of this vile stuff and is responsible for the tragedies which are daily reported in the press occurring in all parts of the country. This piece of legislation, intended to regulate the manufacture, sale and use of alcoholic liquors, can only be enforced in spots, and at great cost to the government. The more vigorously it is enforced, the more antagonistic will the people become to it who believe that it is an invasion upon their personal rights and liberties which cannot or ought not to be infringed upon by puritanic legislation of which the Volstead act is a fair sample. The attempts to carry out its provisions can do no other than to create in the minds of the people the strongest opposition and the determination to get rid of it at some time in the future. The way to make a bad law odious is to enforce it. The Volstead law is becoming more odious day by day and is making liars and hypocrites, snakes and snakes and crim-

regions.label: doc_title
regions.text:
Beautiful, Soft,
Silky Hair
Overnight
Rura KINK-OUT

regions.label: text
regions.text: trails of a good many people who would like to be good citizens, but cannot because they must have their booze or a substitute for it. And this they will get despite the Volstead act and the government booze hounds. The Democrat wet plank is sure to bring out the reserves and they may help to land Al Smith in the Governor's chair because there are so many thirsty ones among them who resent the attempt of government to regulate their appetites and trench upon their personal liberties. Let the good work of enforcement go on and the people will ultimately become real angry and sweep this law out of the constitution to the scrap heap among other fool laws which have excited their derision and forced them to work for their repeal by unity of action and resistance to their enforcement. A horse may be led to the water trough, but he cannot be forced to drink.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: HOWARD ELEVEN SWAMPS NORTH CAROLINA AGRICULTURAL AND TECHNICAL COLLEGE SCORE OF 40—0

regions.label: text
regions.text:
WASHINGTON, D. C., Oct. 25.—The Howard University football eleven in its second game of the season, played Saturday afternoon, October 21, 1922, o the university campus, swamped the North Carolina Agricultural and Technical Colege team, of Greenaboro, N. C. by a score of 40 to 0. The "Blue and White" eleven outclassed the visitors in every department of the game and won about as it pleased.

The game began with Howard kicking off to the Carolinians. After about three minutes of play, Conte, left half-back for Howard, recovered a fumble made by the Carolinians with the ball on their 25-yard line and sprinted away for a touchdown. The attempted drop kick by Doneghy failed. Howard kicked off again and downed the A. and T man on the run-back a. About the same distance from the North Carolinians' goal line as the position of the ball when the costly fumble was made. Following a couple of plays A. and T. kicked out, but the ball was brought up some distance by Howard and after a series of line plunges was worked across the North Carolinians' goal line, being carried across by Doneghy. Howard's right halfback. This time Doneghy's drop kick registered for the additional point.

Howard kicked off to A. and T. for the third time in the first quarter, the ball going all the way down the field across the North Carolinians' goal line. The Howard boys followed up the ball so speedily that before an A. and T man could recover it Doneghy fell upon the ball for another touchdown. Doneghy tried another drop kick for the extra point but failed.

Beginning with the second quarter the North Carolina boys started out in what seemed would be fruitful style. By the use of a trick delayed pass which caused the player taking the ball to use the opposite direction from

regions.label: text
regions.text:
his interference they succeeded in making a couple of good gains. After making use of the play twice, however, the Blue and White eleven caught on to t and successfully broke it up. To - the end of the quarter the North Carolina boys by means of a number of penalties got far down into Howard's territory. After being held for three downs a placement kick was tried, which was blocked and recovered by "Bull Dog" Williams, Howard's famous left end and captain of the team, who in his old style galloped down the field for some 10 yards to A. and T.'s goal for a touchdown. Doneghy by drop kick secured the additional point.

With about six minutes to play, after having received the kick-off from the Carolinians and bringing the ball back up the field by a number of line drives to the visitors' 35-yard line, Coach Morrison sent in his second team. The North Carolina boys again attempted their delayed pass play, which, as in the case of its use against the first team, momentarily nonplussed Howard's second team. But after permitting the A. and T. boys to gain two first downs by this play, the second team boys caught on and broke it up. When the referee's white's blew announcing the end of the first half the ball was in Howard's possession.

The third quarter was uneventful, being spent mainly in punting from one team to the other. The fourth quarter netted Howard two touchdowns, one being secured by Melton, left halfback, who also assisted in getting the back additional point by receiving the well directed forward pass from Carter, Howard's star quarterback. The other touchdown was secured by Cornish, left tackle, who intercepted a forward pass tried by the Carolinians and paced away for their goal. The additional point in this case was made by Carter, quarterback, by the use of a drop kick.

Much surprise was expressed by the large crowd of spectators that the Howard eleven showed such amazing activity in the game after having ap-

regions.label: text
regions.text:
peared so slow in its first game, having won its first game by a score of only 8 to 4. The showing of the team, however, was gratifying to its coaches and followers. After the mediocre opening game the "Blue and White" came to life and played with plenty of dash and precision.

Lineup and summary:
Howard (40) N. C. A. & T. (0)
Williams (Capt.) L E. Dalany
Cornlah. L T. Evans
Nurse. L G. Hayman
Blackwall C. Patterson
Smith. R G. Palmer
Dokes. R T. Spawling
J. Long R E. Brown
Contee. L H B. Embell
Donegby R H Q. Lane
Molson F B. Howell (Capt.)
Carter Q. Tibbell

Score by periods:
Howard 19 7 0 14-40
N. C. A. & T. 0 0 0 0-0
Touchdowns-Contee (1), Doneghy (2), Cornlah (1), Williams (1), and Melton (1), for Howard.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: PROF. SUAREZ SPEAKS AT 135TH ST. "Y"

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Dr Bernardo Ruiz Suares will speak at the West 135th Street T. M. C. A. on Tuesday evening. November 7 at 8 p.m.

Dr Suares is the author of several books on Negro life in the Spanish countries, and his latest book, "The Color Question in the Two Americas," has attracted wide attention in both North and South America.

We are expecting a large and appreciative attendance at the lecture by Dr. Suares.
